coredata.dc:description: In the current study, a fast, ecological, one-pot, and cost-efficient method was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using plant wastes. This method includes the treatment of cantaloupe seeds and mush by microwave for four-minutes as a re-cyclable, green, and cheap carbon and nitrogen source. The furnished N-CQDs show a fluorescence quantum yield of 13.15 %, excellent water solubility, and high stability. A full characterization of the plant waste-derived quantum dots revealed the self-doping with nitrogen. The synthesized N-CQDs have been applied as an efficient nanoprobe for fluorometric determination of nizatidine (NZT), a gastroprotective drug, at λex/λem of 325/416 nm within the concentration range of 0.75–100.0 μM and LOD of 0.24 μM. The N-CQDs show excellent selectivity for NZT in the presence of various possibly co-existing substances. Excellent mean % recovery was achieved for the determination of NZT in capsules (101.97 ± 0.91 %). Moreover, the N-CQDs were used for the determination of NZT in human urine within the concentration range of 2.5–100.0 μM with LOD of 0.8 μM, therefore it has been applied for monitoring the excretion profile of NZT in urine. The greenness of the proposed probe has been evaluated using two greenness software and metrics proving excellent greenness feature that is attributed to using a renewable and cheap plant waste product as a feedstock via low-energy/low-cost microwave-assisted synthesis, elimination of organic solvents and hazardous chemicals, and relying on a direct mix-and-read assay.

Graphical presentation of the synthetic procedure used for the synthesis of fluorescent N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush as a starting renewable substrate using MW at 700 W for 4 min followed by successive sonication, filtration, and centrifugation, showing the chemical structure of NZT (left).

Characterization of N-CQDs: (a) TEM image with the inset showing a histogram of the particle sizes distribution, (b) FT-IR, (c) Zeta potential distribution curve, (d) A co-plot for excitation/emission spectra of the prepared N-CQDs, N-CQDs UV-absorptions spectrum, and NZT. (e) Emission spectra of N-CQDs using λex 300–400 nm. (f) Effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, and (g) Effect of solvent type on the fluorescence of N-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs with different concentrations of NZT (0.75–100.0 μM) with the corresponding calibration curve in the inset. (b) Application of N-CQDs for determination of different concentrations of NZT (2.5–100.0 μM) in urine samples and the corresponding calibration curve in the inset. (c) Cumulative urinary excretion curve of NZT after a 150 mg oral dose (each result is the average value obtained for 3 volunteers).
(a) ComplexGAPI and (b) AGREE results for the evaluation of the greenness of the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Valorization of cantaloupe waste for green microwave-driven synthesis of N-self doped CQDs as a fluorescence sensor for nizatidine in urine and pharmaceuticals. A step ahead for circular economy practice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Mansoura 35516, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, a fast, ecological, one-pot, and cost-efficient method was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using plant wastes. This method includes the treatment of cantaloupe seeds and mush by microwave for four-minutes as a re-cyclable, green, and cheap carbon and nitrogen source. The furnished N-CQDs show a fluorescence quantum yield of 13.15 %, excellent water solubility, and high stability. A full characterization of the plant waste-derived quantum dots revealed the self-doping with nitrogen. The synthesized N-CQDs have been applied as an efficient nanoprobe for fluorometric determination of nizatidine (NZT), a gastroprotective drug, at λex/λem of 325/416 nm within the concentration range of 0.75–100.0 μM and LOD of 0.24 μM. The N-CQDs show excellent selectivity for NZT in the presence of various possibly co-existing substances. Excellent mean % recovery was achieved for the determination of NZT in capsules (101.97 ± 0.91 %). Moreover, the N-CQDs were used for the determination of NZT in human urine within the concentration range of 2.5–100.0 μM with LOD of 0.8 μM, therefore it has been applied for monitoring the excretion profile of NZT in urine. The greenness of the proposed probe has been evaluated using two greenness software and metrics proving excellent greenness feature that is attributed to using a renewable and cheap plant waste product as a feedstock via low-energy/low-cost microwave-assisted synthesis, elimination of organic solvents and hazardous chemicals, and relying on a direct mix-and-read assay.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials grasped the vast attention of researchers, offering attractive opportunities in limitless fields such as bioimaging, disease diagnosis and treatment, food industry, drug delivery, cosmetics, catalysis, semi-conductors, and electronics. Nanomaterials extends a high level of efficiency and performance compared to classical materials by virtue of their distinctive characteristics such as unique optical properties, high surface area, high thermal and electrical conductivity, and excellent mechanical properties. Among the different types of nanomaterials, the carbon-based family has been greatly investigated for numerous purposes and vast applications. In this domain, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new kind of nanocarbons that have zero-dimensional structureand excellent qualities such as tunable excitation-dependent fluorescence, good water solubility, safety, biocompatibility, biodegradability, facile functionalization, and cost-efficiency [1].

Since its first development, a variety of methods have been designed for the synthesis of CQDs including electrochemical methods, chemical routes, thermal combustion, and solvothermal synthesis. Yet, these techniques involve certain limitations such as the need for a very high temperature, very lengthy synthesis, and the use of some harmful chemicals [2]. Thus, there was a strong need for new eco-friendly techniques to overcome these major demerits. For this purpose, microwave (MW) hydrothermal technique was developed as an energy-saving method that offers an excellent and greener route for very rapid synthesis of CQDs without emitting much heat in the environment. MW-assisted technique speeds up and enhances the synthesis kinetics by supplying direct energy to the reactants in a short reaction time compared with traditional heating. This technique offers cost-efficient, environmentally benign, safe, and very effective route for timely preparation of CQDs [3].

In this study, we proposed a new technique for green and eco-effective synthesis of CQDs via MW hydrothermal treatment of Cantaloupe (Cucumis melo var. cantalupensis) [4] wasted seeds and mush as a carbon feedstock. The use of natural wastes and by-products arises as an opportunity for transforming valueless wastes into high-valued CQDs that can be used as a sensitive fluorescence probe. This implemented approach shows many novelty aspects and excellent greenness feature originates from greening the synthetic route, greening the carbon substrate, and making use of worthless wastes together with using a domestic and widely available MW for accomplishing the synthesis process instead of autoclave, furnace, or high temperature reactors commonly used by researchers for nanomaterial preparation. In addition, it is an unexpensive approach and ideally very suited to laboratories with limited resources.

Cantaloupe is a popular fruit crop grown in different regions of the world. In 2020, the global production of cantaloupe totaled 20milliontons, with China, Turkey,Iran,Egypt, andIndia as the significant countries growing cantaloupe. It is characterized by its sensory attributes and high nutrients contents. The non-edible parts of cantaloupe represent a significant portion that is discarded during processing either on the household scale or industrial scale. No studies have been conducted on the use of cantaloupe seeds to produce valued nanomaterial. This encouraged us to use the often-discarded seeds and mush of cantaloupe as a building block for the synthesis of CQDs since they are rich in fatty acids, vitamins, polyphenols, flavonoids, fibers, carbohydrates, and proteins [4,5]. The obvious richness of cantaloupe seeds in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing components implies the prospect of using it for the synthesis of self-doped N-CQDs as carbon and nitrogen source allowing self-doping. The prepared N-CQDs are strongly fluorescent and water soluble which promoted its use as a fluorescent probe. Therefore, the prepared N-CQDs were used for quantitative determination of the H2-blocker nizatidine (NZT).

NZT, N-[2-[[[2-[(dimethylamino)methyl]-4 thiazolyl]methyl] thio]ethyl]-N-methyl-2-nitro-1,l-ethenediamine [6], is used as a gastroprotective agent to inhibit histamine-induced peptic ulcers, conditions related to increased gastric acidity, and reflux esophagitis. Recently, NZT has been considered as the drug of choice for the long-term treatment of hyperacidity and for the management of recently healed active duodenal ulcers [7,8]. Different analytical techniques were reported for the determination of NZT including, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6,9–11], spectrophotometry [12], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [13], spectrofluorimetry [14,15], and electrochemical methods [16]. These methods either employ plentiful organic solvents, require chemical derivatization, or need experienced operators, meanwhile, some are time-consuming which limits their wide applications. Taking these demerits into account, fluorometric analysis showed many significant advantages such as easier operation in shorter time, higher sensitivity, and lower cost. Therefore, a green fluorescence nanoprobe was optimized for the analysis of NZT using N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush as a fluorescence sensing system through synergistic quenching mechansim of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic quenching. It was evidenced that increasing concentrations of NZT results in a proportional quantitative quenching in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. This behavior is attributed mainly to the IFE by NZT that absorbs the UV light at the excitation wavelength range of the N-CQDs, thus it behaves as a filter and attenuates the fluorescence of N-CQDs. We also found dynamic quenching through collision of NZT with N-CQDs in the excited state causing them to return to the ground state. This synergetic mechanism allowed more sensitive determination of NZT.

Thus, in this study an ultrafast MW-induced synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush has been optimized and the prepared N-CQDs have been fully characterized. The fluorescence of the prepared N-CQDs was tailored and they were applied as an efficient and sensitive nanoprobe for quality control of NZT in dosage forms as well as to monitor its cumulative urinary excretion profile in Egyptian volunteers. A full validation study was completed, and the analytical method was evaluated for its environmental friendliness.

A Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, USA) fitted with a Xenon flash lamp and operated at a scan speed of 600 nm/min and a sensitivity of 800 V, with 5 nm slit width for excitation and emission monochromators was used for measurement of fluorescence. UV–VIS absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). A centrifuge (model 2-16P, Germany), thermostatically controlled water bath (Cambridge Ltd, UK), and Eumax ultrasonic bath (model SS 101H 230, USA) were used. We used a Smart SM-2316MH microwave 700 W (China) for the synthesis of N-CQDs. pH measurements were performed using a Consort NV P‐901 pH meter (Belgium).

All chemicals and solvents of analytical grade were used. Sodium hydroxide (98 %), sodium chloride (99 %), methyl cellulose (99 %), magnesium sulfate (99.5 %), potassium chloride (99 %), glucose (99.5 %), sucrose (≥99.5 %), sodium benzoate (99 %), sodium citrate tribasic dihydrate (≥99 %), diclofenac sodium (≥98 %), and quinine sulfate (90 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., U.S.A. Sodium acetate (98 %), boric acid (99 %), sodium phosphate monobasic monohydrate (≥98 %) and acetic acid glacial (99.85 %) were obtained from Adwic Co. (Cairo, Egypt). NZT pure standard (98 %), lot No. ALR6837, was purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Ltd. (Osaka, Japan). Diazepam (99 %) was kindly donated by Nile Co. for pharmaceuticals and chemical industries, Cairo, Egypt. Paracetamol (99 %) was donated by EIPICO Co. (10th of Ramadan City, Egypt). Phenobarbital (99 %) was donated by Memphis Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Ulcfree® capsules containing 150.0 mg NZT/capsule (Eva Pharma Co, Cairo, Egypt) were purchased from a local Pharmacy.

An aqueous solution containing 0.04 M orthophosphoric acid, 0.04 M acetic acid, and 0.04 M boric acid was used to prepare Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) then adjusting its pH with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to pH 2–12.
NZT stock solution (50.0 mM) was prepared in distilled water and further diluted with the same solvent to prepare working standard solutions of NZT (0.5 and 5.0 mM).

The proposed method was applied to a urinary excretion study of NZT in human urine approved by the institutional Research Ethics Committee at Faculty of Pharmacy-Mansoura University (Code No.2023–162). A written informed consent was obtained from the volunteers. The participated volunteers are healthy, non-smoking one male (37 years old-85 Kg) and two females (26 years old-75 Kg and 28 years old-73 Kg) with no history of allergy to NZT. Volunteers were instructed to abstain from any medicines a week before the experiment and to overnight fast before administration. The obtained urine samples were collected and stored at − 20 ◦C until analysis. Pre-dose urine samples were collected from a volunteer and used for the initial preparation of the calibration graph.

Fresh cantaloupe was brought from a local supermarket in Mansoura City, Dakahlia, Egypt. The cantaloupe fruit was cut into two halves and the inner seeds and mush were scooped out and thoroughly mixed with a domestic fruit mixer to obtain a homogenous mixture. Then, the obtained mixture was passed through gauze cloth to get rid of the particulate matters. Twenty grams of the obtained paste was accurately weighed and transferred to a small beaker, treated with MW radiation in a household microwave at 700 W for 4 min, then allowed to cool to room temperature. Twenty mL of distilled water were added to the obtained black residue followed by sonication for 30 min, then filtration. The filtrate was centrifuged for 20 min at 6000 rpm and the supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.45 µm syringe filter. The obtained dark brown solution was kept in an amber-colored bottle in the refrigerator (4 °C).

The characteristics of the prepared N-CQDs have been explored by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) to investigate the chemical structure of the synthesized CQDs using Thermo-Fisher Scientific Nicolet—iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. Moreover, transmission electron microscopy imaging (TEM) using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) working at 200 kV was used. Additionally, Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical, Ltd, Worcestershire, UK) was used for determination of the zeta potential of the CQDs. The optical properties of the quantum dots, such as florescence and absorbance, were also examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield Ф = 54 %) [17] was used as a reference to determine the quantum yield (Ф) of the synthesized N-CQDs. The integrated fluorescence of standard solutions of quinine sulfate and N-CQDs (with absorbance less than 0.05 at λex, to minimize IFE) has been estimated. The Ф of N-CQDs has been calculated applying Eq. (1) [17]:

Where, F = the integrated fluorescence, A = absorbance, and n = the refractive index of the solvent. The symbols (x) and (St) indicate the N-CQDs and standard quinine, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of calibration curve for NZT
ce:section.ce:para: Increasing volumes of NZT standard solution (0.5 mM) were transferred to a group of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks, then 1.0 mL of N-CQDs and 1.0 mL of BRB (pH 8.0) were added. Distilled water was added to complete the final volumes to 10 mL and the solutions were well-mixed. The fluorescence (F) was measured at 416 nm after excitation at 325 nm. A blank experiment was performed, and its fluorescence (F0 ) was recorded. Each experiment has been carried out three times. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting Ln (F0/F) against NZT concentration [μM] and the linear regression equation has been calculated.

Ten Ulcfree® capsules (labelled to contain 150 mg NZT/capsule) were carefully emptied and their content was accurately weighed. An accurately weighed amount of the homogenized powder was dissolved in distilled water to prepare a solution containing 0.5 mM NZT. The mixture was sonicated for 20 min and filtered to obtain a clear solution. Afterwards, increasing volumes of the obtained solution have been transferred to a group of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks and the previously mentioned method in section “2.7.1.” was applied to determine NZT concentrations. The percent recovery and standard deviation were figured out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for NZT determination in urine
Construction of calibration curve for NZT in urine
ce:section.ce:para: Urine samples were collected from a healthy volunteer who abstained from any medicines and caffeine to perform the study. For determination of NZT in urine samples, a reported protein precipitation method [18] was followed with some modifications. 1.5 mL ethanol and 0.3 mL of urine were transferred into a series of screw capped centrifugation tubes. Increasing concentrations of NZT were added and the volume was completed to 3.0 mL using distilled water. The tubes were centrifuged for 20 min at 6000 rpm, then 0.2 mL of the obtained supernatant was carefully transferred to a series of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks, followed by 1.0 mL of BRB (pH 8.0) and 0.5 mL of N-CQDs. Distilled water was added to complete the volumes to 10 mL. A blank experiment was performed simultaneously excluding NZT and its fluorescence (F0 ) was recorded. Each experiment was performed three times, and the calibration curve has been constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of NZT in real urine samples from volunteers
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy overnight fasting volunteers administered one Ulcfree® capsule containing 150.0 mg NZT. Urine samples were collected at different time intervals starting from zero-time up to 6 h and the volume of urine was measured after each collection. The urine samples were kept at −20 °C until analyzed after gentle thawing. 0.3 mL of each urine sample were transferred to a screw-capped centrifugation tube followed by 1.5 mL ethanol and the volumes were completed to 3.0 mL with distilled water. The tubes were centrifuged for 20 min at 6000 rpm and 0.2 mL from each supernatant was carefully transferred to a 10.0 mL volumetric flask. The same steps mentioned in section “2.8.1” were done. The fluorescence was measured at 325/416 nm and the derived regression equation (Section 2.8.1.) was used to calculate the concentration of NZT in each sample.

The amount of NZT excreted at each time interval (t) (Ut ) was calculated using the following equation [19]:

The cumulative urinary excretion curve of NZT was established by diagramming the % dose excreted(t) at each time interval versus time (h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MW-assisted Synthesis of waste-derived N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush
ce:section.ce:para: A single-step MW hydrothermal approach was utilized for the green synthesis of waste-derived N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush. When cantaloupe seeds and mush were held for 4 min in the MW at 700 W, blue luminescent N-CQDs were synthesized. The prepared N-CQDs exhibited a strong fluorescence at λexcitation/λemission of 340/416 nm, respectively. The main components of cantaloupe seeds are oil (30.65 %), protein (27.41 %), carbohydrate (29.96 %), fibers (25.32 %), and considerable amounts of phenolic compounds. Thus, nitrogen and oxygen-rich CQDs resulted from the consecutive dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and surface self-passivation in the presence of the carbonaceous organic components [4,5].

The MW time needed for preparation of the CQDs has been studied by exposing the cantaloupe seeds and mush to MW radiation at 700 W for different time intervals (2–8 min). The study revealed that the irradiation time that yields quantum dots with the highest quantum yield was 4 min as shown in Fig. 1 a.

Moreover, to ensure the reproducibility of the proposed synthesis method and its suitability for real application with available materials, seeds and mush from another variety of melons, namely honeydew melon, have been examined as a starting feedstock for the synthesis of CQDs. Both cantaloupe and honeydew are melon varieties belonging to the species Cucumis melo. We found that the quantum dots prepared from honeydew seeds and mush showed very similar optical properties to those prepared using cantaloupe seeds (Fig. 1b) showing similar quantum yields with %RSD not exceeding 1.5 %. This similarity in the optical characters of the prepared CQDs is attributed to the similar components of the seeds of the two plant varieties [20].

Additionally, the robustness and reliability of the synthesis procedure was studied by using cantaloupe collected from different areas in Egypt and at different times: winter (October), autumn (September), and summer (August), as a source of seeds used in the synthesis procedure. No significant change is observed in the fluorescence emission wavelengths for all the investigated alternates (Fig. 1c & 1d). Besides, cantaloupes collected from different Egyptian regions, and cantaloupes collected in different seasons showed reproducible and robust results confirmed by the small values of relative standard deviations (%RSD) among the prepared N-CQDs (2.3, and 1.2 %, respectively).

These findings guarantee the suitability of the developed synthesis approach for application in different laboratories using accessible materials. The proposed synthesis procedure, as briefly illustrated in Fig. 2 , proved to be superior to the reported hydrothermal methods used for preparation of N-CQDs since it is very rapid, consumes low energy, and takes place in a single step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the prepared N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and morphological aspects of the prepared N-CQDs were investigated by TEM imaging. The obtained TEM image (Fig. 3 a) shows that the synthesized CQDs evenly distributed, well separated from each other, and displayed a sphere-shaped morphology. The particle size histogram shows that the average particle size of the CQDs is 12.9 ± 2.7 nm.

Furthermore, the functional groups and the composition of the prepared N-CQDs have been explored using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 3b). The spectrum revealed a broad peak at 3386 cm−1 which is attributed to O-H and N-H stretching. Additionally, a peak appears at 2317 cm−1 is probably attributed to CC stretching. A peak at 2157 cm−1 was assigned NC and NNN stretching. Moreover, the spectrum displayed a peak at 1990 cm−1 which was attributed to CCC stretching and N-H bending. Finally, a peak at 1632 cm−1 denotes CC stretching, CO stretching, or NH bending [21]. The high polarity and water solubility of the synthesized CQDs was achieved by the abundant inclusion of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of CQDs that was proved by the FTIR spectra. The surface charge of the N-CQDs was determined by evaluating its Zeta potential (ζ) that was found to be −30.2 mV (Fig. 3c). The negative value of ζ is attributed to the surface function groups and it explains the stability of the synthesized N-CQDs due to their repulsion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization and quantum yield of the prepared N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the synthesized N-CQDs were studied using UV–VIS spectrophotometer. The absorption spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 3d) revealed a band at 300 nm which is attributed to n–π* transition of the surface carbonyl/amine functional groups and another band at 215 nm referring to π-π* transition of the CC of the CQDs’ aromatic core [22].

Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of the synthesized N-CQDs have been investigated. Upon excitation at 340 nm, an intense blue fluorescence was obtained at 416 nm (Fig. 3d). Besides, the N-CQDs displayed excitation-dependent emission when applying λex over a range of 300–400 nm as shown in Fig. 3e. The obtained spectra show that increasing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 340 nm resulted in a corresponding increase in the fluorescence intensity (FI) while the emission wavelength is almost the same. Further increase of the excitation wavelength from 350 to 400 nm led to a regular decrease in FI of the corresponding emission spectra with continuous shifting of the emission wavelength to longer wavelength. This excitation-dependent emission property resembles the behavior of CQDs reported in literature [22] and theories explained it by the presence of multiple surface states, wide size distribution, formation of carbon nuclei in CDs, and slow solvent relaxation around CDs [23]. Additionally, this tunable excitation-dependent emission permits the wide use of the synthesized N-CQDs as an excellent fluorescent probe for various substances [24].

Applying λex/λem of 340/418 nm yielded the highest FI. The superior fluorescence capabilities of the prepared N-CQDs were confirmed by measuring its quantum yield (QY) with respect to the standard fluorescent quinine sulfate. The QY was found to be 13.15 % which proved the excellent fluorescence property of the synthesized N-CQDs from cantaloupe seeds and mush and their preference over many reported sources [25–34].

The effect of pH on the fluorescence of the synthesized N-CQDs has been investigated over the pH range of 2.0–12.0 using BRB (Fig. 3f). It was noticed that the FI is nearly constant over the pH range of 2–6 showing a slight gradual decrease at higher pH values (Fig. 3f). Increasing the pH in the alkaline side (pH > 6.0) leads to electron transfer from the electron lone pair of surface amino group to the CQDs which leads to gradual decreasing fluorescence. In contracts, the lone pair of electrons of the amino group is not available for transfer when protonation takes place in acidic pH, thus the fluorescence intensity is higher under acidic condition (pH < 6) [35].

The influence of solvents on the fluorescence properties of the synthesized N-CQDs has been explored using water, methanol, ethanol, and acetonitrile (Fig. 3g). It is noted that the solvent type significantly alters the FI of N-CQDs, while the impact of solvent type on maximal emission wavelength is not significant. The lowest fluorescence intensity was observed in the aprotic solvent acetonitrile, while the protic solvents; water, methanol, and ethanol; resulted in higher FI. The N-CQDs have abundant surface oxygen and nitrogen functional groups available for H-bonding with protic solvents. This solvent attachment to the edge of the N-CQDs provides emissive traps within the molecular structure results in higher FI [36,37].

Moreover, the photostability of the N-CQDs was examined by exposing it to a constant UV-radiation at 365 nm. It was found that after 30 min, only 5.5 % decrease of the fluorescence intensity took place. Additionally, the N-CQDs solution displayed good stability for at least 1 month without any instability sign as turbidity, aggregation, precipitation, or floatation, where the change in the fluorescence quantum yield did not exceed 1.4 % after 1 month storage at 4 °C. Thus, it is noteworthy that the prepared N-CQDs have excellent stability qualifying it for numerous applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity MTT assay against WI-38 normal cell line
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of N-CQDs against normal WI-38 cell line (fetal lung fibroblast cells) was evaluated using MTT test. MTT assay is a colorimetric assay based upon a mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase enzyme that yields purple formazan derivatives from the yellow tetrazolium bromide (MTT) in viable cells. The MTT assay was performed following the reported procedures [38]. The % relative cell viability was calculated using the following Eq.:
(5)
% Re l a t i v e
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
Absorbance
of
treated
samples / A b s o r b a n c e
o f
u n t r e a t e d
s a m p l e
× 100
Doxorubicin was used as a reference cytotoxic agent (IC50 of 2.60 ± 0.05 μg/mL) [38]. The results shown in Table S1 indicate that the N-CQDs almost have no cytotoxicity against normal WI-38 cell line with very high IC50 values (83.74 ± 4.2 μg/mL), compared to very low IC50 value of doxorubicin. Additionally, the N-CQDs showed 100 % cellular viability at concentrations below 6.25 μg/mL as shown in Table S1. These results displayed the safety and non-toxicity of the prepared N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the developed microwave-assisted synthesis of waste-derived N-CQDs and reported methods for synthesis of quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Since the first uses of MW in chemistry were reported in 1986, it founds copious applications in what is called “MW-assisted synthesis” [39]. The use of MW radiation for quantum dots synthesis accelerates the synthesis process, thus reducing the required time from several hours to only few minutes while offering a simple and facile synthesis platform with unique green character [40]. Herein, we explored cantaloupe wasted seeds and mush as an alternative viable input for sustainable production of CQDs. The CQDs recycled from cantaloupe waste present an innovative advance to not only evade critical impacts on the environment, but to also execute circular economy practices, which are fundamental to achieve sustainable development.

The proposed MW-assisted synthesis of the waste-derived CQDs proved its prevalence over the reported methods [25–34,41–44] due to its easy application, dependence on easily accessible domestic MW, cost-efficiency, and enhanced greenness of the method by using plant waste. In additon, the incorporation of various functional groups in the prepared N-CQDs, the high quantum yield, the good stability of the produced CQDs, and their excellent fluorescent properties are significant merits. On the other hand, the reported synthesis methods require very long time and very high temperature [26–34,43,44] (Table S2).

The suggested procedure is 30–360 times quicker than the reported hydrothermal methods and 30 times faster than the dry heat methods [33,34] (Table S2). Furthermore, the proposed technique offers an ecofriendly method that utilizes fruit waste as a carbon/nitrogen precursor without using any hazardous chemical and with least energy consumption (Table S2) to synthesize fluorescent N-CQDs with QY higher than [25–34] or comparable to the reported method [41–44] for CQDs synthesis. The slightly higher QY of some of the reported CQDs is attributed to the variable nature of the precursors. For example, the use of pure chemicals such as β-alanine and L-ascorbic acid [41], or natural product such as walnut oil [43] rich in polyunsaturated fatty acid, or onion/cabbage juices [42] rich in protein and sulfur compounds furnished CQDs with QY somewhat higher than the prepared N-CQDs. However, the suggested method helps to boost the green feature and makes full use of the plant waste which is a key advantage of the synthesis procedure. Furthermore, our method is 360 and 45 times faster than these methods [43,44], respectively. Eventually, the designed approach offers a fruitful method to add value to plant wastes obtained either from household practice or industrial business.

The applicability of the synthesized N-CQDs has been tested as a fluorescence sensor for determination of NZT. Since NZT shows UV-absorption maximum at 325 nm (Fig. 3d), we got the benefit of the tailored excitation-dependent emission behavior of the prepared CQDs to be used as a fluorescence probe for sensing NZT. This was achieved by adopting excitation/emission wavelengths of CQD at 325/416 nm, depending on fluorescence quenching by primary inner filter effect (IFE) that comes to effect when the quencher (NZT) exhibits UV-absorption at the excitation wavelength of the N-CQDs (Fig. 3d) [45]. It was found that, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs at 416 nm was obviously quenched after addition of increasing concentrations of NZT (0.75–100.0 µM) as shown in Fig. 4 a. The N-CQDs fluorescence decreased by about 80 % by addition of NZT (100.0 µM).

To confirm the mechanism of N-CQDs’ fluorescence quenching by NZT, we have investigated the incorporation of IFE as a key mechanism of fluorescence quenching. Primary and secondary IFE originate when a quencher shows UV-absorption at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorescent species, respectively [45]. As seen in Fig. 3d, the absorption spectrum of NZT (at pH 8.0) overlapped greatly with the excitation spectrum of the synthesized N-CQDs, while NZT shows no interference with the emission spectrum of N-CQDs. Thus, it is concluded that primary IFE (pIFE) has a significant role in fluorescence quenching.

In order to verify the proposed mechanism, FI values have been corrected by removal of IFE to calculate the corrected fluorescence (Fcorrected ) using Eq. (6) [45,46]:
(6)
F

F

× a n t i l o g (

+

)
Fcorrected defines FI after exclusion of IFE, Fobserved denotes the observed FI, and Aex and Aem are the absorbance of NZT at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-CQDs, respectively.

Afterwards, the % suppressed efficiency (%E) for the corrected and observed FI was calculated using Eq. (7) [45,46]:
(7)
% E = [ 1 -
(
F
F
0
)
] × 100
Therefore, the role of IFE was confirmed by the great decline in %E upon subtracting the IFE since about 66 % of the %E was lost by exclusion of IFE (Fig. S1a).

Thus, further investigations were conducted to explore the role of other fluorescence quenching mechanisms like dynamic or static quenching. Dynamic quenching involves the decrease of fluorescence intensity due to the collision between quencher and fluorophore in its excited state and it is enhanced by raising the temperature. Whereas the static quenching occurs due to complex formation between the quencher and fluorophore in its ground state, and it is negatively affected by increasing the temperature [46]. Hence, a Stern-Volmer study was performed to distinguish whether any of the two mechanisms has a role in N-CQDS fluorescence quenching. The Stern-Volmer plots were constructed at three increasing temperature settings (310, 320 and 330 K). Plots of
F
0
F
versus [NZT] have been constructed (Fig. S1b) according to Eq. (8) [47].

(8)
F
0
F
= 1 + K
[ N Z T ]
Where K = Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [NZT] = the molar concentration of NZT.
As illustrated in Table S3, the K increases with increasing the temperature, confirming that dynamic quenching is responsible for the remaining part of fluorescence quenching.

From the results of mechanism study, it is concluded that the quenching of N-CQDs fluorescence resulted from a combination of pIFE and dynamic quenching. This synergetic mechanism allowed the sensitive determination of NZT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical method optimization for NZT determination by the N-CQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Various parameters were examined to determine the optimum conditions for NZT determination using the synthesized N-CQDs. These conditions involve the pH, volume of buffer, and volume of N-CQDS. BRB with pH ranges from 2.0 to 12.0 was used to investigate the effect of pH on fluorescence quenching (Fig. S2a). The value of F0/F increased steadily with increasing the pH over the range (2.0–8.0) followed by a gradual decrease at higher pHs (9.0–12.0). Therefore, pH 8.0 was chosen as the ultimate pH for the analysis of NZT by N-CQDs. The buffer volume has been studied over the range of 0.5–2.0 mL. Fig. S2b demonstrates that the impact of buffer volume is not extreme. Thus, 1.0 mL of BRB (pH 8.0) was selected as the optimum volume in this study.

Additionally, the effect of N-CQDs volume on F0/F has also been explored over the range of 0.5–2.0 mL. It was concluded that using 1.0 mL of the N-CQDs had the best quenching effectiveness as shown in Fig. S2b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the analytical method as per ICH guidelines
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of the proposed method was evaluated according to the ICH guidelines for analytical method validation [48]. Ln (F0/F) values [49] showed good linearity with different concentrations of NZT within the concentration range of 0.75–100.0 µM according to the following regression equation:
(9)
L n
F
0
/ F
= 0.0182 x + 0.037
r = 0.9999
Where x represents the NZT concentration [μM] and r refers to the correlation coefficient. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were also calculated and found to be 0.24 and 0.73 μM, respectively which confirms the high sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor.

Moreover, the accuracy and precision of the proposed method have been investigated on inter- and intra-day levels. Three concentrations of NZT were analyzed three times in the same day and for three successive days to evaluate both intra- and inter-day precision, respectively. The high values of %found and small values of %RSD indicate the high precision of the proposed method (Table S4).

The USP official HPLC method [6] was applied for determination of NZT to compare its results with that obtained using the developed method using Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test. It is notable from Table 1 that, there is no significant difference between the results of the two methods where the calculated t- and F-values are lower than the theoretical values [50].
The obtained results prove that the analysis using the synthesized N-CQDs obeys all validation criteria of the ICH guidelines [48] confirming its suitability to be used as a fluorescence probe.

The selectivity of the prepared N-CQDs was evaluated to assess its applicability in the presence of different species such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, citrate, methyl cellulose, glucose, sucrose, sodium benzoate, ammonium oxalate, ammonium chloride, urea, and uric acid. Additionally, some drugs that are likely to be co-administered with NZT, such as paracetamol, diazepam, diclofenac, and phenobarbital were investigated. The results shown in Table S5 reveal that these species have no considerable effect on the FI of the N-CQDs since the %change in presence of such species ranged from + 4.17 to −2.54 % proving the selectivity of the sensor and its capability for pharmaceutical and biological analysis.

The proposed N-CQDs fluorescent sensor has been utilized for determination of NZT in commercially marketed capsules using three different concentrations of NZT. The high %found (100.71–102.53 %) with small SD value (±0.91) (Table 1) settled the ability to use the synthesized N-CQDs for pharmaceutical applications without any interferences from common excipients or additives. Furthermore, the results for determination of NZT in capsules by the proposed method were compared with the results obtained by the USP official HPLC method [6] by Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test. No significant difference was observed between the two methods regarding accuracy and precision (Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NZT determination in urine samples and figuring out the cumulative urinary excretion curve of NZT
ce:section.ce:para: Since the efficiency of the N-CQDs to selectively determine the concentration of NZT has been proven, it was applied for determination of NZT in human urine. Furthermore, a matrix-matching calibration has been applied to eliminate the matrix effect from the urine components. According to the guideline on bioanalytical method validation [51], the calibration standards should be prepared in the same matrix as the matrix of the intended study, i.e. in urine. Thus, the calibration curve (Fig. 4b) has been constructed in urine by graphing Ln F0/F versus [NZT] showing excellent linearity (r = 0.9998) over the concentration range of 2.5–100.0 µM with excellent % recoveries (84.16–116.94 %). (Table 2 ). The LOD and LOQ values were also calculated and found to be 0.81 and 2.45 µM, respectively. Moreover, intra-, and inter-day accuracy and precision have been examined for determination of NZT in urine by analysis of three different concentrations within the same day and on three successive days, respectively. The high %recovery and small values of RSD, % for intra-day precision (≤1.71 %) and inter-day (≤2.31 %) indicated the high accuracy and precision of the developed method (Table 2).

Therefore, the developed fluorescence probe has been established to be reliable and robust for monitoring the cumulative urinary excretion of NZT after a single oral dose in Egyptian volunteers. As reported [52], NZT is completely absorbed following oral administration and it is excreted primarily through the kidneys (65 % to 90 %). The present study showed that approximately 77 % of the administered dose of NZT has been excreted in urine within six hours (Fig. 4c). The obtained results showed excellent agreement with the reported values for NZT urinary excretion [52]. This confirms the suitability of the synthesized nanoprobe for analysis of NZT in real and practical samples selectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method greenness assessment and circular economy practice contribution
ce:section.ce:para: Green chemistry is vital for management of the levels of environmental pollution as well as resolution of global environmental problems. Therefore, the development and evaluation of green analytical methods that depend on using renewable green reagents, eliminating the use of harmful chemicals, and consuming minimum energy becomes a must. In this context, different tools have been designed for assessment of the greenness of the analytical methods [53] such as complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [54] and Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE) [55] approaches.

ComplexGAPI software [54] is specially designed for evaluating the greenness property of analytical methods that involve the use of materials synthesized prior to the analytical step, such as N-CQDs in the proposed method. The lower hexagon in the pictogram shown in Fig. 5 a corresponds to the level of greenness of the synthesis step. It involves the evaluation of the conditions, safety and health hazards, purification, and work up. The upper five pentagons demonstrate the green features of the analytical method involving reagents, procedures, and equipment, recognizing various factors such as quantity, health effect, safety of reagents and solvents, intake of energy, production of waste, and nature of the method. The red, yellow, and green colors indicate high, medium, and low environmental risks. The obtained results revealed that both procedures used in the synthesis of N-CQDs, and the analysis satisfies many green requirements as shown in Fig. 5a.

Additionally, the greenness of the developed method was further evaluated using the AGREE approach [55]. This approach also quantifiably assesses the greenness of the developed procedure by determining the amounts, toxicity, waste released, energy consumed, and the number of applied steps. As shown in Fig. 5b, the score resulted was 0.75, which proves the greenness of the proposed method.

Insights on the reasons for this greenness feature revealed that the developed approach possesses many green values that help in implementing the sustainability concept to analytical chemistry laboratories. First, relying on a simple, low energy-consuming, and very fast method for the synthesis of waste-based N-CQDs using clean MW radiation. Second, utilization of cantaloupe waste product as a green renewable source for obtaining the N-CQDs helped to enhance the greenness of the proposed method while converting a low value by-product into a value-added product. Third, cost-efficiency qualifies the developed method for wide application in low-income laboratories. Fourth, safety for humans and the environment. Fifth, exclusion of hazardous chemicals and organic solvents. Sixth, a green solvent, ethanol, has been used for protein precipitation prior to urine analysis to conserve the method’s greenness and safety. Comparing the developed method to other hydrothermal methods shows that this method is cost-effective, less energy-consuming, and less time-consuming (Table S2).

Notably, the developed approach contributes to the concept of circular economy by reducing material use, recapturing waste for creating a further value and production of new materials. In turn, this protects the environment, reduces material dependency, and saves money.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the N-CQDs fluorescence probe and the reported methods for NZT
ce:section.ce:para: A comparison between the efficiency of synthesized N-CQDs as a fluorescence sensor and the reported analytical procedures for NZT determination [6,9–17] has been conducted (Table S6). It is obvious that the reported methods are time and energy consuming. Additionally, some methods used perilous chemicals or organic solvents such as N-bromosuccinimide, diethylamine, triethylamine, 1-dimethylaminonaphthalene-5-sulphonyl chloride, phloroglucinol, methanol, acetic acid, chloroform, and acetonitrile, or heating at high temperatures. On the other side, the proposed method evades such chemicals and harsh conditions.

The proposed method has a comparable sensitivity to the reported HPLC method [10], and higher sensitivity than the reported spectrophotometric method [12]. Despite the reported spectrofluorimetric method [14] showed better sensitivity than the present method, it needs multiple steps including derivatization, reaction time of 30 min, extraction of reaction product with chloroform, followed by fluorescence measurement. Similarly the reported spectrofluorimetric method of Aboul-Enein et al. [15] requires complexation reaction at 60 °C for 15 min prior fluorescence measurement. In contrast, our method is a mix-and-read approach since the reaction is immediate between NZT and N-CQDs, and no harmful chemical is involved in the entire procedure. Although the LC-MS method [13] is more sensitive than the proposed method, it requires highly experienced person and sophisticated and very expensive device.

In this study a new ultrafast and highly efficient procedure has been executed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent N-self doped CQDs. For the first time, cantaloupe seeds and mush have been utilized as a green, cheap, renewable, and widely available carbon precursor with N-self doping capacity. Cantaloupe is a widely cultivated fruit that makes it very convenient for the synthesis of N-CQDs. The MW-based synthesis method is rapid, one-pot, simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly where the synthesis has been established at 700 W within 4 min without using any chemicals or solvents. The synthesized CQDs were fully characterized using TEM, Zeta potential measurement, fluorescence, UV–VIS, and IR spectroscopy. Moreover, the synthesized N-CQDs had high fluorescence quantum yield of 13.15 % with good water solubility and stability. Therefore, the synthesized N-CQDs became a superior tool as a pharmaceutical sensor. The N-CQDs sensor has been applied for the determination of NZT in pharmaceutical capsules within the concentration range of 0.75–100.0 μM with % recovery ± SD of 100.62 ± 1.13. Since the proposed method proved its excellent performance and selectivity during NZT determination, it was used to study NZT excretion pattern in human urine showing that 77.0 % of the administered dose has been excreted within 6 h. Ultimately, the developed analytical method showed excellent greenness as it is based on a plant waste product, it is devoid of any hazardous chemicals, organic solvents, high temperature, or time consumption. Importantly, the developed method is considered a step-ahead for applications of circular economy practices in research laboratories, that is very suited for limited resources institutes.
